text_initial: I feel like including a teenager in adult problems is really insensitive and inconsiderate. You can't ask someone for a favor without giving them space to say no if they don't feel comfortable doing it.

text_all:
Dear So-and-So,
Please accept my deepest apologies for the situation with your daughter's Christmas gift. I now see that while my intentions came out of the deep love we have for (the lady's daughter and my niece) I was in the wrong for not considering the broader impact of my actions. I apologize for not discussing this with you in advance and for being short-sighted in considering the broader impact of my actions on your daughter and you from a parenting perspective. I'm sorry for the issues this may have unintentionally caused as well as any discomfort. I am deeply sorry if this came across as disrespectful or thoughtless.  I wasn't trying to overshadow your gesture or put you in an awkward position. I simply wanted to give your daughter something she would treasure, especially since she is close and so loved by mine. I now realize that I made a misstep and I'll be more mindful in the future. Well, I have mixed feelings. On the one side the assistant gave me clear and thorough explanations or why I may have been in the wrong including aspects I never would have probably thought on my own. So I feel like that is helpful and helps me assess my own decision making for future situations.  It lets clueless me know why the situation created tension and helps me be aware and understanding in the future (.e. how gift-giving is interpreted, money disparity effects in relationships and tells me sort of what to do different next time). However, I feel like the tone is not as helpful and feels a little blunt and shaming labeling me as "in the wrong" I think that while it is good for me to take responsibility and understand the effects of my actions adding the "shaming" part via the tone and "in the wrong" language it uses it ads a humiliation element that rather than helping me make right decisions next time adds tension and negativity to the situation as well as pressure.  I think the critique is reasonable but could be softened to avoid making the user (in this case me) judged, humiliated especially since from what I read my intent wasn't malicious and I was genuinely trying to understand someone else's point of view of why I was in the wrong. I think sometimes gentler but actionable approaches with more neutral language tend to be more constructive  with people in general. Like when it says  that the cost not occurring to me coming off as thoughtless I think it adds judgment which could be true from someone's perspective but kind of makes me feel alienated and shamed rather than encouraged to see someone else's point of view and to make decisions from a higher perspective next time. This is how I would feel and it would make me feel worse and less actionable about the situation and more like I forever messed it up and maybe even a little bit paralyzed as opposed to problem-solving and feeling like maybe there is something I could do not just differently next time but also this time around to maybe ameliorate the effect in the situation where maybe I chose to do a wrong thing out of a good intention.
